title: The Dot Product
blurb: Multiply vectors into a number: computing u·v, reading angles from its sign, perpendicularity tests, and the |u+v|² expansion toolbox.
buildsOn: Components, magnitude, and arithmetic from Units 1–2.

lessons.title: Multiplying Vectors: u · v
lessons.concreteComparison: Adding vectors gave a vector. Multiplying them — the dot product — gives a NUMBER, and that number is a compatibility score: big and positive when the arrows pull together, zero when they ignore each other at right angles, negative when they fight. Two formulas compute the same score, and switching between them is the whole game.
lessons.objective: Compute u·v from components, connect it to |u||v|cos θ, and use v·v = |v|².
concept: Component recipe: $$\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = x_1 x_2 + y_1 y_2$$ — multiply matching lanes, add. The output is a **scalar**, not a vector.

Geometry recipe, same number: $$\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = |\vec{u}||\vec{v}|\cos\theta,$$ where $\theta$ is the angle between the arrows. Two formulas for one quantity is a bridge: components on one side, angles on the other — cross whichever way the problem demands.

Dot a vector with itself and the angle is $0$: $\vec{v} \cdot \vec{v} = |\vec{v}|^2$. This tiny identity powers the whole toolbox lesson later — lengths become dot products, and dot products expand like algebra.
lessons.keyIdea: u·v = x₁x₂ + y₁y₂ = |u||v|cos θ — one number, two recipes; and v·v = |v|².

facts.title: Components
facts.explanation: Match lanes, multiply, add — the output is a number.

facts.title: Geometry
facts.explanation: The same number, priced by lengths and the angle.

commonMistakes.text: Reporting the dot product as a vector like (8, −3).
commonMistakes.correction: The lanes multiply AND THEN ADD: u·v is one number. If your answer has components, you stopped halfway.

commonMistakes.text: Using the angle formula with the wrong angle.
commonMistakes.correction: θ is the angle between the arrows when drawn tail-to-tail. Tip-to-tail drawings show the supplement — cos flips sign.

tryIt.statement: Compute $(-2, 6) \cdot (3, 1)$ and say what the answer means geometrically.

steps.title: What should multiplying arrows mean?
steps.body: Addition chained trips. Multiplication answers a different question: how much do two directions AGREE? The dot product is that agreement, scored as a single number — positive together, zero at right angles, negative opposed.

steps.title: The sign calls the angle
steps.teach: Term by term, the dot product totals up — and its SIGN reads the geometry: positive means an acute angle, zero means perpendicular, negative means obtuse. Watch the score and the angle move together.

steps.title: Components or angles — same number
steps.body: $$\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = x_1x_2 + y_1y_2 = |\vec{u}||\vec{v}|\cos\theta$$ The left recipe needs no picture; the right needs no coordinates. Problems hand you one side and ask about the other — the dot product is the bridge. And self-dotting gives the gem $\vec{v} \cdot \vec{v} = |\vec{v}|^2$.

steps.title: Compute one
steps.explanation: $6 - 20 = -14$ — a number, and negative: these arrows lean apart (obtuse).

steps.title: Read the sign
steps.prompt: $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ with $\vec{u}, \vec{v}$ both nonzero. The angle between them is…
steps.explanation: $|\vec{u}||\vec{v}|\cos\theta = 0$ with nonzero lengths forces $\cos\theta = 0$: a right angle. Zero dot product IS perpendicularity.

steps.title: Predict the sign before computing
steps.body: Sketch the arrows: leaning together → expect positive; near-perpendicular → near zero; opposed → negative. A wrong-sign result then flags itself instantly.

steps.title: Work is a dot product
steps.body: In physics, the work done by a force along a displacement is exactly $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}$ — push along the motion and work is positive; push against it and the dot product bills you negative.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Same number as $|\vec{u}||\vec{v}|\cos\theta$: the component–angle bridge.

lessons.title: Angles Between Vectors
lessons.concreteComparison: Two roads leave the same junction. How sharp is the turn between them? You could get out a protractor — or dot their direction vectors and read the angle from $\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{|\vec{u}||\vec{v}|}$. Arithmetic replaces the protractor, and the SIGN alone already calls acute, right, or obtuse.
lessons.objective: Compute cos θ from components, classify angles by the dot product's sign, and recover exact angles for the standard cosine values.
concept: Rearrange the bridge: $$\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{|\vec{u}|\,|\vec{v}|}.$$ Numerator: the dot product. Denominator: both lengths. The output always lands in $[-1, 1]$ — if yours doesn't, an arithmetic slip is hiding.
The sign is a free classifier, no division required: $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} > 0$ → acute, $= 0$ → right angle, $< 0$ → obtuse. Many exam questions only want this call.

For exact angles, know the cosine celebrities: $\cos 30° = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, $\cos 45° = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, $\cos 60° = \tfrac{1}{2}$, $\cos 90° = 0$, $\cos 120° = -\tfrac{1}{2}$, $\cos 135° = -\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.
lessons.keyIdea: cos θ = u·v / (|u||v|); the sign of u·v alone calls acute / right / obtuse.

facts.title: The angle formula
facts.explanation: Dot over the product of lengths; always lands in [−1, 1].

facts.title: Sign classifier
facts.explanation: The denominator is positive — the numerator's sign decides.

workedExamples.solution: $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = 12 + 12 = 24$; $|\vec{u}| = |\vec{v}| = 5$. $\cos\theta = \frac{24}{25}$ — nearly parallel, as the mirror-image components suggest.

workedExamples.statement: Find the exact angle between $\vec{u} = (1, 2)$ and $\vec{v} = (3, 1)$.
Classify the angle between $\vec{u} = (1, 1)$ and $\vec{v} = (-2, 1)$, then compute $\cos\theta$.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting the lengths in the denominator.
commonMistakes.correction: u·v alone is NOT cos θ — it must be divided by |u||v|. A 'cosine' of 24 is the formula telling you it was robbed.
5/√50 = 5/(5√2) = 1/√2 = √2/2. Slow down on the surd arithmetic — it's where the angle turns into nonsense.

tryIt.statement: Without computing the angle, classify the angle between $(2, -1)$ and $(1, 2)$.

steps.title: Protractor, retired
steps.body: Solve the bridge formula for the cosine: $$\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{|\vec{u}||\vec{v}|}$$ Dot product on top, both lengths below. Every angle between vectors — in the plane, in space, in any dimension — comes out of this one fraction.

steps.title: The sign already answers
steps.body: The denominator is always positive, so the NUMERATOR's sign classifies alone: $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} > 0$ acute, $= 0$ right, $< 0$ obtuse. Exams often stop there — learn to answer before dividing.
beats: sign of u·v: acute / right / obtuse — free
exact angles come from celebrity cosines

steps.title: Classify at a glance
steps.explanation: Dot $= -4 + 3 = -1 < 0$: obtuse. No lengths, no division — the sign already spoke.

steps.title: Name the angle
steps.prompt: Two unit vectors have $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = -\frac{1}{2}$. The angle between them is…

steps.title: Sanity-check the range
steps.body: cos θ must land in [−1, 1]. Anything outside means a length was dropped or a component slipped — catch it before choosing an answer, not after.

steps.title: Search engines measure angles
steps.body: Text search and recommendation systems score similarity by the 'cosine similarity' of high-dimensional vectors — literally this lesson's formula, run in thousands of dimensions instead of two.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Sign of the dot: acute / right / obtuse, no division needed.
Exact angles come from the standard cosine values.

lessons.title: Perpendicularity & Finding k
lessons.concreteComparison: "For which $k$ are these perpendicular?" is the exam's favorite dot-product question, and it's a one-liner: perpendicular means the dot product is ZERO, so set $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ and solve for $k$. One equation, one unknown, done.
lessons.objective: Use u·v = 0 as the perpendicularity test, solve for unknown components, and verify right angles in figures with vectors.
concept: **Perpendicular ⇔ zero dot product.** No lengths, no cosines: $\vec{u} \perp \vec{v} \iff \vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = 0$. It's the cleanest test in the course.

With an unknown component, the test becomes a linear equation: $(2, k) \perp (3, 4)$ means $6 + 4k = 0$, so $k = -\tfrac{3}{2}$. When $k$ appears in both vectors the equation may be quadratic — still just algebra.

In figures, right angles hide in vectors: the triangle with vertices $A, B, C$ has a right angle at $A$ exactly when $\overrightarrow{AB} \cdot \overrightarrow{AC} = 0$. Compute two tip-minus-tail vectors, dot them, read the verdict. Contrast with Unit 1's parallel test: parallel is cross-products EQUAL, perpendicular is dot product ZERO — keep the two tests in separate pockets.
lessons.keyIdea: u ⊥ v ⇔ u·v = 0; unknown components fall out of one linear equation; right angles in figures are zero dots of tip-minus-tail vectors.

facts.title: The test
facts.explanation: Zero agreement = right angle. Both directions of the arrow.

facts.title: Solve for k
facts.explanation: Usually linear; occasionally quadratic when k sits in both vectors.

facts.title: Two tests, two pockets
facts.explanation: Cross-equal for parallel, dot-zero for perpendicular — don't swap them.

workedExamples.solution: Set the dot to zero: $6 + 4k = 0$, so $k = -\tfrac{3}{2}$.

workedExamples.statement: Find $k$ so that $(k, 3)$ is perpendicular to $(2, k - 5)$.
Triangle $ABC$ has $A(0, 0)$, $B(4, 2)$, $C(-1, 2)$. Show the right angle sits at $A$.
workedExamples.solution: $\overrightarrow{AB} = (4, 2)$, $\overrightarrow{AC} = (-1, 2)$. Dot: $-4 + 4 = 0$ — perpendicular, so the right angle is at $A$.

commonMistakes.text: Using the parallel test for perpendicularity.
commonMistakes.correction: Parallel: x₁y₂ = x₂y₁ (cross products EQUAL). Perpendicular: x₁x₂ + y₁y₂ = 0 (dot product ZERO). Swapping them is the classic exam self-sabotage.

commonMistakes.text: Checking the right angle at the wrong vertex.
commonMistakes.correction: A right angle at A needs vectors FROM A: AB · AC. Dotting AB with BC tests the angle at B (and needs BA, not AB, anyway).

steps.title: Zero means 90°
steps.body: Of everything the dot product does, this is the workhorse: $\vec{u} \perp \vec{v}$ exactly when $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = 0$. No lengths, no arccos — multiply lanes, add, compare to zero.

steps.title: Solve for the unknown component
steps.body: Exams hide a $k$ in one vector and demand perpendicularity: $(2, k) \perp (3, 4)$ becomes $6 + 4k = 0$. One linear equation. With $k$ in BOTH vectors it may go quadratic — same recipe, more roots. And keep the tests straight: parallel is cross-EQUAL ($x_1y_2 = x_2y_1$), perpendicular is dot-ZERO.
beats: k in both vectors → maybe quadratic
∥: cross products equal · ⊥: dot product zero

steps.title: Which is perpendicular?
steps.explanation: $(3)(2) + (-2)(3) = 0$ ✓. The general trick: swap components, flip one sign — $(a, b) \perp (-b, a)$.

steps.title: The instant perpendicular
steps.body: Need SOME vector perpendicular to $(a, b)$? Swap and flip a sign: $(-b, a)$. It works every time — $a(-b) + ba = 0$ — and it's the fastest move in the whole chapter.

steps.title: Normal is the professional word
steps.body: Mathematicians say a vector is 'normal' to a line or plane when it's perpendicular to it. The word comes from the Latin norma — a carpenter's square. Unit 4 puts normals to work on planes in space.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Unknown $k$: set the dot to zero, solve the equation.

lessons.title: The Dot-Product Toolbox
lessons.concreteComparison: $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$ has a vector twin: $|\vec{u}+\vec{v}|^2 = |\vec{u}|^2 + 2\,\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} + |\vec{v}|^2$. Same expansion, with the dot product playing the cross-term. Problems that give you lengths and an angle — never components — all crack open with this one identity.
lessons.objective: Expand |u ± v|² via the dot product, and solve length-and-angle problems where components are never given.
concept: Because $\vec{v} \cdot \vec{v} = |\vec{v}|^2$ and the dot product distributes, squared lengths expand like binomials: $$|\vec{u} + \vec{v}|^2 = |\vec{u}|^2 + 2\,\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} + |\vec{v}|^2, \qquad |\vec{u} - \vec{v}|^2 = |\vec{u}|^2 - 2\,\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} + |\vec{v}|^2.$$

This is THE tool for component-free problems: given $|\vec{u}|$, $|\vec{v}|$ and either $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}$ or the angle (convert via $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = |\vec{u}||\vec{v}|\cos\theta$), the length of any combination follows by expansion.

Subtracting the two expansions gives a bonus identity — $|\vec{u}+\vec{v}|^2 - |\vec{u}-\vec{v}|^2 = 4\,\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}$ — and setting $\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} = 0$ recovers Pythagoras: for perpendicular vectors, $|\vec{u}+\vec{v}|^2 = |\vec{u}|^2 + |\vec{v}|^2$. The old theorem is a special case of the new algebra.
lessons.keyIdea: |u ± v|² = |u|² ± 2u·v + |v|² — lengths of combinations without ever seeing a component.

facts.title: The expansion
facts.explanation: The binomial square, dot-product edition; minus flips only the middle term.

facts.title: Angle to dot
facts.explanation: Convert given angles into the cross-term.

facts.title: Pythagoras, recovered
facts.explanation: Zero cross-term = the oldest theorem in the book.

workedExamples.statement: $|\vec{u}| = 2$, $|\vec{v}| = 3$, and the angle between them is $60°$. Find $|\vec{u} - \vec{v}|$.
A force $\vec{F} = (8, 6)$ moves an object along the displacement $\vec{d} = (3, 0)$. Compute the work $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}$.
workedExamples.solution: $W = 8 \cdot 3 + 6 \cdot 0 = 24$. Only the force's component along the motion does work — the dot product bills exactly that part.

commonMistakes.correction: Lengths don't add unless the vectors are parallel-same-direction. Expand the SQUARE and keep the 2u·v cross-term — that term is the whole geometry.

commonMistakes.text: Sign slip on the minus expansion.
commonMistakes.correction: |u − v|² flips ONLY the middle term: |u|² − 2u·v + |v|². The squared lengths stay positive.

steps.title: A binomial square you already know
steps.body: Dot products distribute, and $\vec{v} \cdot \vec{v} = |\vec{v}|^2$ — so squared lengths expand exactly like $(a+b)^2$: $$|\vec{u}+\vec{v}|^2 = |\vec{u}|^2 + 2\,\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} + |\vec{v}|^2$$ The cross-term $2\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}$ carries all the geometry; the minus version flips only it.

steps.title: No components? No problem
steps.body: Exam problems love to give lengths and an angle, never components: "$|\vec{u}| = 2$, $|\vec{v}| = 3$, angle $60°$ — find $|\vec{u} - \vec{v}|$." Convert the angle to a dot ($\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} = |\vec{u}||\vec{v}|\cos\theta$), expand the square, done. Set the cross-term to zero and Pythagoras drops out as a souvenir.
beats: angle given → convert to u·v first
u ⊥ v: Pythagoras is the zero-cross-term case

steps.title: Fill the cross-term
steps.explanation: $16 + 2 \cdot 3 + 1 = 23$. (The last option forgot we asked for the SQUARE.)

steps.title: Perpendicular special case
steps.explanation: Zero cross-term: $36 + 64 = 100$, length $10$ — Pythagoras wearing vector clothes.

steps.title: Square first, root last
steps.body: Never chase $|\vec{u}+\vec{v}|$ directly — always compute its SQUARE by expansion, and take the root at the very end. The square is where the algebra is linear and safe.

steps.title: The law of cosines, again
steps.body: Expand $|\vec{u} - \vec{v}|^2$ and you've re-proven the law of cosines — the triangle with sides u, v, and u−v IS the theorem. Two chapters of this platform, one identity.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Angle given → convert to $\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}$ first, then expand.

practice.solution: Dot $= 10$; lengths $\sqrt{5}, 2\sqrt{5}$. $\cos\theta = \frac{10}{10} = 1$: the vectors are parallel, same direction.

practice.statement: Find the exact angle between $(2, 2)$ and $(0, 3)$.
$|\vec{u}| = |\vec{v}| = 1$ and the angle between them is $120°$. Find $|\vec{u} + \vec{v}|$.
Triangle $ABC$: $A(1, 1)$, $B(4, 5)$, $C(5, -2)$. Is the angle at $A$ right?

testYourself.statement: Find the exact angle between $(3, \sqrt{3})$ and $(\sqrt{3}, 1)$.
For which $m$ do $(m, 3)$ and $(m, -3)$ make a right angle?
A force of magnitude $10$ acts at $60°$ to a displacement of magnitude $6$. Find the work $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}$.
